item.title: LLM Dependence
item.description:
“But I Don’t Want to Become Dependent”

The most common objection I hear about working with LLMs is the fear of dependency. People worry that if they lean on an AI for implementation, research, or thinking through problems, they’ll lose the ability to do those things themselves. The concern is reasonable and misses the point.

Managers don’t worry about “becoming dependent” on their team. A director does not lie awake wondering whether she’s atrophied because she no longer makes the financial models herself. She has a different job now. Her effectiveness is measured by the quality of the questions she asks, the clarity of the priorities she sets, and whether the people working under her have what they need to do good work. The tool changed. The standard of contribution changed with it.

This is the shift that LLMs are forcing on knowledge workers, and it explains why the technology makes people uncomfortable in a way that earlier tools did not: LLMs remove the excuse that busyness was ever a substitute for judgment.

We Are All Managers Now

If you accept the premise that an LLM is less like a calculator and more like a very fast, somewhat unreliable junior employee, then the question changes. You stop asking “am I becoming dependent?” and start asking the questions any competent manager would ask:

Have I given this subordinate what they need to meaningfully do the job?

Good managers don’t hand someone a vague assignment and then blame them for the result. They provide context, constraints, and clear success criteria. The same discipline applies to a prompt. If your LLM is producing mediocre work, the first place to look is the quality of the briefing you gave it.

Is the work I’m delegating the highest-value use of this resource?

Time and attention are finite. A manager who has her best engineers fixing formatting issues is wasting capacity. Likewise, if you’re using an LLM to do things you could do in three minutes, while keeping all the heavy work for yourself at 11 PM, you’ve made a poor allocation decision. Not because you’re wrong to be distrustful of LLMs output, but because you’ve abdicated the important decision of “What can I reasonably delegate to an LLM and still expect a good outcome?”.

Does the output meet the standard?

Managers are accountable for deliverables, regardless of who (or what) produced it. When you delegate implementation to an LLM, you haven’t delegated responsibility. You still need to evaluate the result, catch errors, and decide whether the work is good enough to put your name on.1 Conversely, you don’t have the luxury of an ego if you’re committed to being a good manager: your performance is measured by other people’s output.

Your biggest prerogatives are: “How do I get better, sustainable performance out of the people and technology available to me?”, and “How do I improve my team’s capability to create sustainable value for the organization?”.

The Spotlight Problem

When an LLM handles the implementation — the boilerplate code, the first draft, the research synthesis — what remains is the work that only you can do. And that work is harder to fake.

The real discomfort with LLMs is about losing deniability.

The questions that matter now are the ones most people have been deferring: - Am I meaningfully creating value for my organization, or am I staying busy? - What does success look like for this project, and how do I know if were off track? - What did I learn today that I didn’t know yesterday, and how/if does that change my direction?

These are executive-level questions. They always were. The difference is that before LLMs, the volume of implementation work gave most people a plausible reason not to sit with them. That reason is gone. The implementation can be delegated. What cannot be delegated is the judgment about what to implement, why it matters, and whether it’s working.

The WiFi Test

There’s a revealing moment that happens when someone who works heavily with LLMs loses their internet connection. If the first reaction is “I can’t work,” it means something has gone wrong — not with the tool, but with the relationship to it. A manager whose team is out of the office for a day doesn’t stop being useful. She uses the time to think about the things she’s been too busy to address:

  • Are we working on the right problems?
  • What should we stop doing because it no longer adds value?
  • Where am I trying to be in six months, and does this week’s work have any connection to that?

The person without WiFi and a notes app has everything they need for the most important part of the job. The LLM can wait. The thinking shouldn’t.

References

Footnotes

  1. This happened in the news recently. It’s one of many examples where lawyers are no longer the only ones who are ending up in the news for blindly relying on LLMs.↩︎

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: The Self-Fulfilling Prophecy
item.description:
How Organizations Destroy the Leadership Capacity They Claim They Never Had

Peter Drucker spent decades arguing that the purpose of management was to make people’s strengths productive. He did not say to inventory weaknesses and design around them. He did not say to sort employees into “thinkers” and “doers” and then optimize the doers out of existence. But that is what a growing number of organizations are doing with large language models, and the consequences will take years to surface and a generation to repair.

Long Before the Technology…

The pattern is old, and the reasons behind it were never irrational. Long before LLMs, most organizations confined junior staff to process work: formatting slides, pulling data, writing first drafts that a senior person would rewrite from scratch.

There were legitimate reasons for this. Junior employees make mistakes that cost the organization time, money, and sometimes client relationships. A senior consultant’s hour is billed at four or five times what a junior’s is, and spending that hour coaching someone through a bad deliverable has a real opportunity cost. Clients expect polished output, not developmental exercises. Managers are evaluated on quarterly results, not on whether their direct reports will be ready for a leadership role in 2031.

So the junior employee learned to execute tasks and received limited exposure to the reasoning behind those tasks. What exposure they did get came through informal, often uncomfortable channels. A senior partner’s sense of what the client would accept was rarely written down, because writing it down would have required an honesty that nobody wanted on record. What clients say they value and what they actually buy are frequently at odds: a client might insist they want rigorous analysis and then approve the deck that tells them what they already believe. A client might ask for innovation and then reject anything unfamiliar.

These contradictions are well understood by anyone who has spent years in a client-facing role, but they are understood as instinct, as feel, as judgment. They are not understood as rules that could be shared during onboarding, partly because they resist codification and partly because documenting them would be embarrassing for everyone involved.

So juniors learned these heuristics the way apprentices did: by proximity. They sat in on meetings and watched which slides the partner skipped. They got the draft back covered in red track-changes with the explanation: “this isn’t what they want.” Sometimes the lesson arrived as an email at 10 PM that communicated, in its own blunt way, a standard that no onboarding document had ever articulated.

After two or three years of this, leadership would observe that select members of the junior cohort were beginning to show a degree of savviness. They would be promoted to senior associate and become responsible for overseeing the cycle perpetuate itself. Others would leave for greener pastures elsewhere.

This was always a problem. What LLMs have done is force it into view.

The Assumption Behind the Pitch

The sales pitch for LLM adoption in organizations runs roughly like this: your people spend too much time on low-value tasks. Drafting, summarizing, researching, formatting, synthesizing. Let the model handle these things so your people can focus on higher-order work.

And the pitch is grounded in something real. Organizations are under genuine pressure. Margins are tighter than they were a decade ago in most professional services. Clients increasingly expect faster turnaround at the same or lower cost. The labor market for skilled junior talent is competitive and expensive. If a tool can produce an acceptable first draft in thirty seconds instead of three days, the business case for adopting it is straightforward, and the manager who ignores it will lose ground to the one who doesn’t. These are not foolish people making careless decisions. They are responding rationally to the conditions in front of them.

But there are assumptions buried in the pitch that deserve examination. The first is that the “low-value” tasks and the “higher-order” work are cleanly separable, that you can remove one without damaging the other.

The second is subtler and, I think, more consequential. The pitch assumes that someone can tell the model what “good enough” looks like. An LLM produces output in response to a prompt, and the quality of the output depends on the specificity of the instruction. But the standards that governed most knowledge work were never specific. They were aesthetic. A senior partner knew what the client would accept because she had spent fifteen years reading the room, absorbing reactions, learning that this client hated bullet points and that client wouldn’t read anything longer than two sentences.

These preferences were rarely consistent and never fully articulated. To use an LLM effectively as a replacement for junior work, someone would need to translate that accumulated, contradictory, largely unspoken sensibility into written instructions precise enough for a model to follow. The organizations that are adopting these tools fastest are, in most cases, the organizations that have invested the least in codifying what good work actually means for their specific clients. They are handing the model a task and a vague standard, and then asking a junior employee, the person with the least client exposure, to judge whether the output meets it.

Neither assumption holds up well under pressure. Those consequences will surface in time.

What “Low-Value” Work Actually Teaches

Consider what a junior employee actually learns when they draft a deliverable badly. They learn what questions the senior reviewer asks. They learn which assumptions get challenged and which get waved through. They learn to anticipate objections, to notice when data doesn’t support the conclusion they want, to distinguish between an argument that persuades and one that merely sounds persuasive. The deliverable itself may be mediocre. The learning is not.

When an LLM drafts that deliverable instead, the output may be better on the surface. The sentences will be clean. The structure will be logical. But the junior employee has learned nothing about leadership, because they didn’t do any. They’re sitting in an awkward middle-seat: responsible for work that was completed by an LLM while likely insulated from the client’s feedback through their manager. Drucker understood something about knowledge work that most productivity discourse ignores: the development of capability happens through iterative decisions and feedback about the work itself.

You do not first acquire judgment and then apply it. You acquire judgment by making hard decisions, badly at first, with feedback from people who are more experienced at it. An organization that removes this developmental work in the name of efficiency has made a choice about its future, whether it realizes it or not.

It has chosen to stop cultivating its own strategic thinkers.

The Manufactured Talent Shortage

And here the prophecy fulfills itself. Leadership says: our junior people can’t make decisions, so we’ll automate the routine tasks and reserve decision-authority for seniors. The junior people, now deprived of the work and the decision-making experience around it predictably fail to develop it. A decade or so later, when a senior person leaves or retires, there is nobody ready to step into the role, because nobody was given the opportunity to grow into it. The organization has manufactured the talent shortage it thought it was responding to.

This problem compounds in a specific way I think organizations are unprepared for: when you automate the execution layer, the strategic burden doesn’t disappear. It shifts downward. Tasks that used to be routine now require supervision, curation, judgment calls about when the model’s output is good enough and when it has gone wrong in ways that require domain knowledge to detect. The people asked to make these calls are the same people who were never experienced in making them.

The decision-making will be harder for people with the least experience making hard decisions.

We recognize this as a management problem, even if not a management failure in the conventional sense. The individual decisions are rational. The system they produce is not. The technology does what it does. The question is what management decides to do with it. And the decision most organizations are making, often by default rather than by design, is to treat LLMs as a substitute for human development rather than as a complement to it. The reason we know this? Because LLM companies are marketing their services in terms of productivity, emphasis mine:

ChatGPT Enterprise Gemini Enterprise
Sales: Personalize outreach to any customer to close faster Sales:
Legal: Draft and review legal content faster and with more consistency Legal:
Finance: Automate analysis and reporting with confidence Finance:
Operations: Less time on busywork, more focus on what moves the business Operations:

Or as Claude Enterprise says: “Get work done faster”.

The Management Decision

There is a different path, but it requires something most organizations find difficult: accepting a short-term cost for a long-term capability that does not show up on this quarter’s scorecard. An “investment” by another name.

A junior analyst who uses an LLM to generate a first draft and then rewrites it with their own judgment, explains their reasoning, and defends their choices in a review meeting is doing more developmental work than one who spent three days assembling the draft from scratch. The tool can compress the mechanical labor. It cannot compress the thinking, and it should not be asked to replace it. The question for management is whether the time saved by the model gets reinvested in developing the employee’s judgment or simply gets absorbed into more output at the same level.

This is not an easy question, and it would be dishonest to pretend otherwise. The organization that invests in development while its competitors use the same tools to cut headcount and lower costs will, for a period of years, look less efficient by any standard measure. The returns on building strategic capacity in junior employees are real but slow and hard to quantify. The returns on increased output are immediate and easy to put in a board deck. Every incentive in the system pushes toward the second option.

Tomorrow’s Consequences, Made Today

Given those incentives, most organizations will choose more output. This is a predictable outcome, not a moral failing.

They will point to short-term productivity gains and declare the investment justified, and by the measures available to them, they will be right. In five to ten years, when they look at their leadership pipeline and find it thin, they will attribute it to the talent market, or the educational system, or generational differences in work ethic. They are unlikely to trace it back to the decision, made today, to remove from junior employees the work that would have prepared them for senior roles: the slow, expensive, difficult process of doing strategic thinking before they were good at it.

The most important decisions are about tomorrow, or more precisely, what we do or fail to do today. The decision to hollow out developmental work is one of those. It is being made now, in thousands of organizations, one “productivity improvement” at a time. And by the time its consequences become visible, the people who made it will have moved on, retired, or been promoted for the short-term efficiency gains they delivered. The people left behind will inherit the vacuum.

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: Stability Is (Was Always) an Illusion
item.description:
The final essay in the series

A Long Time Ago…

In 1812, the London and Westminster Gas Light and Coke Company began laying pipe beneath the streets of London. Within a decade, gas lamps had replaced oil lamps across most of the city’s major thoroughfares. The lamplighters — organized, unionized, well-compensated, socially respected — watched it happen in real time. They petitioned Parliament. They sabotaged gas mains. They argued, correctly, that gas light was dangerous and unreliable and that their expertise in maintaining the city’s lighting infrastructure had been built over generations and could not simply be discarded.

They were right about all of it. And none of it mattered.

Carl Benedikt Frey’s The Technology Trap tells this story alongside dozens of others, and what makes the book so useful isn’t the parade of obsolescence — it’s his careful documentation of what the affected workers actually believed while it was happening. They believed their skills were permanent. They believed the institutions that employed them would protect them. They believed, above all, that stability was a feature of the world rather than a temporary condition maintained by the absence of a better alternative.

Every generation that has lived through a major technological displacement believed theirs was different. The lamplighters believed gas was a fad. The handloom weavers believed Parliament would intervene. The switchboard operators believed automation couldn’t handle the complexity of human communication. Each group had reasonable arguments. Each group was operating with the best information available. And each group was wrong — not because they were stupid, but because they mistook the current arrangement for a permanent one.

A grim way to open a final essay, but stay with me.

The part of Frey’s research that sticks with me is the effect on “identity”. When the handloom weavers lost their trade, they didn’t just lose income. They lost the ability to answer the question “what do you do?” in a way that made sense to them and to the people around them. Their social standing, their sense of competence, their daily routines, their relationships with other weavers — all of it was organized around a skill that the market no longer valued. The economic recovery, when it eventually came, didn’t restore any of that. It replaced it with something different, for different people, in different places.

This is the unspoken conversation when you hear the word “disruption”. The policy conversation is almost entirely about wages, retraining programs, unemployment insurance — the mechanics of economic adjustment. Those things matter. But they don’t address the thing that actually makes displacement so destructive, which is that people build their identities around their work in ways that are deeper than a paycheck.

I’ve fallen into this trap myself. There have been stretches where my sense of who I was had become so tangled up with my productivity that unemployment feels like a personal moral failing. You start measuring yourself by output. You start treating rest as inefficiency. You start interpreting any period of uncertainty as evidence that you’ve been faking it the whole time. It gets dark. The cruelty is that the people most susceptible to this — the ones who care the most about good work — are who it hits hardest.

I bring this up because the current wave of layoffs and restructuring is disproportionately targeting junior and mid-level positions. This makes a certain kind of organizational sense: companies are protecting employees with mortgages, families, deeper institutional knowledge.

The effect is that the people with the least institutional protection are absorbing the most institutional risk.

If you’re 24 and you just got laid off from your first real job, the economic damage is probably manageable. The identity damage is something else entirely. The social contract — go to college, work hard, get a good job, build from there — just broke its promise to you, and no amount of LinkedIn advice about “pivoting” or “upskilling” is going to make that feel less like a betrayal.

So if you’re in that position, or if you’re watching friends go through it, the first thing I want to say is: the grief is real, it’s legitimate, and it isn’t a sign of weakness. Guard yourself as much as you can against the belief that your productivity is your self-worth. Be a good friend. Surround yourself with good friends. Keep pushing even when it feels pointless, because it probably isn’t, even though it will feel that way for a while.

Okay, Matt, that’s pretty dark. Any silver linings?

I’m not going to give you a speech about how you’re actually the protagonist all along. That would be insulting, especially to a generation that held up its end of the social contract and is now watching the other party default. Likewise, Frey’s reporting indicates that the economic effect of displacement for members of the Lamplighters’ Guild was felt for three generations afterwards.

My family imparted two pieces of wisdom that I found deeply annoying at the time and have since come to respect. The first is the worst curse that any human can intone on another: “May you live in interesting times.” The second is career advice: “Great crisis necessitates great opportunity.” These two ideas are in tension with each other, it’s about how we find the balance between them. For me, my first two jobs were in Disaster Risk Reduction and Emergency Management. When the world is structured around interesting times, you might as well get professionally comfortable with them.

This is my observation about the position we’re all in.

We have, right now, the most freedom of movement of almost anyone in the economy. I say that seriously. If we’re students or recent graduates — unmarried, no kids, no mortgage — the number of choices available to us is relatively extraordinary. We can move across the country for a job. We can start a company out of a friend’s apartment. We can take a wild bet on a sector that didn’t exist two years ago. The 45-year-old with a family and a mortgage in a declining industry would trade a great deal to be in our position, even if our position feels precarious and frightening.

That’s the freedom-of-movement argument, and it’s real, but it’s not the most interesting one.

The more interesting argument is about information asymmetry, and specifically about how much of it just collapsed.

For most of modern professional life, there has been an enormous gap between what credentialed experts know and what everyone else can verify. Your financial advisor tells you a particular allocation strategy is optimal, and unless you have a finance degree yourself, your options are to trust them or to spend forty hours reading textbooks. Your lawyer tells you a contract clause is standard, and you either take their word for it or pay a second lawyer for a second opinion. Your consultant tells you their methodology is proprietary and their recommendations are data-driven, and you nod along because what else are you going to do?

LLMs did not close this gap entirely. A chatbot is not a substitute for genuine expertise, and anyone who tells you otherwise is selling something. But LLMs did something almost as consequential: they gave non-experts the ability to ask informed questions. You can take your financial advisor’s recommendation, feed it to an LLM, and get back a list of specific questions that a competent person would ask. You can paste in a contract and get a plain-language explanation of what each clause actually commits you to. You can’t replace the expert, but you can negotiate with them on far more equal footing than was possible even three years ago.

A lot of people have been coasting on the information asymmetry. Not all of them — plenty of professionals are genuinely excellent at what they do — but enough that this shift is going to be visible. When anyone can cross-verify claims at scale, the professionals who were selling confidence rather than competence are going to have a very bad decade. And when large, stable, absurdly high-margin companies start getting disrupted — and some already are (RIP Chegg) — the openings that appear will favor people who can move quickly and think clearly over people who have seniority and inertia.

You can do everything. You can do nothing. You can reinvent yourself with skills and capabilities that previous generations could only imagine. The one thing you cannot do is wait for someone else to tell you the best path. By the time the consensus forms and the dust settles, the opportunity has already been seized by someone who moved while it was still unclear.

Okay, but easier said than done. You’re sitting here with your fancy title and your resume and you can spew whatever you want because you don’t have skin in the game the way we do.

True. And I’ll own that. I have my privileges and my support structure and a long list of people who were generous with me when they didn’t need to be. I have been, on multiple occasions, lucky.

And an honest personal observation: the people I know who’ve navigated periods of upheaval successfully didn’t do it because they had a plan. They did it because they were willing to act before they had a complete picture. They made the best decisions they could under genuine uncertainty, they adjusted when those decisions turned out to be partially wrong, and they kept moving when the temptation was to freeze and wait for clarity.

My grandmother is a successful entrepreneur, founder, and has exited her own company. As much as it drives me absolutely crazy, she has that killer instinct where she can smell opportunity and execute on it before she has a coherent picture of the end-game.

That doesn’t require genius, it requires is a tolerance for discomfort — the willingness to commit to a direction when you can only see about thirty feet ahead, knowing you’ll have to correct course repeatedly and that it will be expensive.

You have even footing with almost everyone. The tools are new enough that nobody has twenty years of experience with them. The people pretending they have all the answers are improvising just as fast as you are. And you have the advantage of not needing to protect a position you’ve already built — you can go wherever the opening is, without having to justify abandoning something you spent a decade constructing.

“May you live in interesting times.”

You do. I do. That’s the hand we’re dealt. The question of what we decide to create in the middle of them is an open one. Nobody is going to hand that to us. But nobody is going to hand it to anybody else, either. The ground is moving underneath all of us, equally. So we do what humanity has done through disruptions and crises since we first walked this spinning globe.

Keep pushing.

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: Good Taste and Experience
item.description:
What’s the most valuable thing in the world right now?1

Not technical skill. Machines have that.

Not information — everyone has access to more of it than they can process.

The answer is judgment.

Specifically, two kinds of judgment that work in tension with each other: Experience and Taste.

Why? LLMs can implement almost anything. They can write code, draft strategy documents, build financial models, design systems. The quality varies, but the range of what’s possible is enormous and getting wider every quarter. So the bottleneck has moved. It’s no longer “can we build this?” It’s “should we build this, and is what we built good enough?” Those are judgment calls, and no model makes them for you.

If you’re early in your career, this means the people who will matter most in any organization, at any level, are the ones who can answer those two questions well.

Knowing When It’s Good Enough

The critical moment when you’re working with an LLM is deceptively simple. You look at what it produced and you make a call: “Not good enough, try again” or “Good enough, move on.” That’s it. Everything can always be improved. The question experience answers is: given the time and resources we actually have, is this sufficient?2

Experience, in this context, means something specific. You need enough accumulated knowledge to define concrete success criteria — even when your tools are limited and your company has cut corners on model access. You need to know what the work should look like before anyone starts doing it.

But Matt, I’m early in my career. I haven’t run enough projects to know what “good enough” looks like.

That’s a legitimate concern, and five years ago it would have been harder to answer. But the cost of accumulating reps has dropped to nearly zero. You can open any LLM right now and start building something. It won’t turn out great — that’s the point. You’ll have a concrete artifact to examine, a set of decisions you made along the way, and a clear record of where those decisions fell short. That’s experience. It compounds.

The constraint most people face early on — how do I make something that creates a tangible impact when nobody’s giving me real projects? — turns out to be the ideal training ground. Experience doesn’t come primarily from the scale of the work. It comes from the frequency of iteration. A person who builds ten small things and reflects on each one will develop sharper judgment than someone who spent six months and only made it half-way through a single deliverable and never looked back. (I have done this. Many times. This is the fifth time I’ve tried to start a blog).

Make the reflection concrete.3 Before you start a deliverable, write down what “done” looks like. As you work, note every point where the criteria shifted — every time you were told to do one thing but the real need turned out to be something else. Then evaluate your output against both your original standards and the twists along the way. You are your own consultant. Nobody else will do this synthesis for you, and the accumulated record of your own judgment calls is more valuable than any course or certification.

So invent a business. Pick an industry relevant to your work, create a fictional company, and put it through its paces. Design a full web application for it. Have your LLM build out a financial and operations model. Then look hard at what came out, identify what went wrong, and do it again. The fictional stakes are low. The judgment you’re building is real.

Taste

Knowing What “Good” Actually Looks Like

Experience tells you what’s achievable given constraints. Taste tells you whether the achievable thing is worth achieving.

Kira Klass defined good taste in business as “the ability to consistently make decisions that demonstrate respect for your audience, show deep empathy for their needs, and deliver aesthetically-pleasing experiences that drive positive business outcomes.”

The negative test is even more useful: you know you have Taste when bad work offends you. Not because you’re being precious about it, but because you can see the gap between what something is and what it should be, and that gap bothers you.

If Experience is the pragmatic half of judgment, Taste is the aspirational half.

Why does Taste decay? Because most professionals stop anchoring what “good” looks like. They spend years processing mediocre deliverables, gradually accepting lower standards as normal. The resume gets longer. The frame of reference gets blurrier. They still say they want great work, but they’ve lost the ability to recognize it when it shows up — or, more importantly, to recognize when it hasn’t.

Kira defined the other side too: bad taste is “characterized by decisions that prioritize short-term gains over user experience, disregard audience needs and preferences, or implement aesthetics that clash with functionality.”

That description covers a lot of what passes for professional output in most organizations. People have been tolerating it for so long that they’ve forgotten there’s an alternative.

The counter is active study. Find work you admire and ask yourself why it’s good — what decisions the creator made, and what they chose not to do. The restraint matters as much as the ambition.

Then produce things and put your name on them.4 A blog, a side analysis, a portfolio project. These aren’t vanity exercises. They’re how you develop standards, because you discover what you’re willing to sign. The bar isn’t perfection. The bar is “I’m not embarrassed by this.” If you can’t work at a pharmaceutical company, you can still write about financial ratios for pharmaceutical companies if that’s what interests you. You can analyze GLP-1 distribution strategy. Something is better than nothing, and a few good things are better than one good thing.5

The Tension

Neither Experience nor Taste is sufficient on its own. They need each other, otherwise the resulting failures are distinct (and memorable).6

When you first start, you’re going to have a lot of self-criticism around Taste and a lot of anxiety around Experience. It’s going to be an uncomfortable first set of steps. That’s okay.7

The people who hold both together will run the next generation of organizations — not because the tools chose them, but because the tools made everyone else interchangeable.

  • The Babysitter. You review every output. You catch what the LLM missed. You say, “No, try it this way instead” and provide a specific suggestion. This works, but it’s labor-intensive. You’re still doing most of the thinking — the LLM is essentially a faster pair of hands.

    The Manager. You define acceptance criteria before the work starts and put an LLM to work on a certain project-milestone to keep things manageable. You evaluate the results and pick the best one. This is a step up, because you’re not babysitting- you’re evaluating outcomes. The reason why it’s not the “best” is because this cycle needs to be done one “milestone” at at time.

    The Orchestrator. An LLM project manager spawns other LLM workers, assigns each one a specific task with clear instructions, lets them work, then tests their outputs against the acceptance criteria you defined. If a solution passes, it generates an after-action report and saves the changes. If it fails, it documents what was tried and hands the problem off to the next instance. Your job at this level is to define success so precisely that the system can evaluate itself without your involvement.

    Notice what happens as you move up the ladder: the demands on your experience increase, not decrease. At Level 3, you need to articulate your standards clearly enough that an automated system can enforce them in your absence. Most professionals have never been forced to be explicit about what “good enough” means. They’ve relied on gut feel and real-time correction. That can’t scale.

  • Taste without Experience produces the junior person with strong opinions and no operational understanding of what it costs to realize them. They can tell you what’s wrong with everything. They can’t tell you what it would take to fix it, or whether fixing it is worth the tradeoff. Criticism without capability is just noise.

Footnotes

  1. Welcome! This is an essay series about how LLMs shifting the mindset around Early Careers, AKA the people who face the greatest amount of institutional risk with the least amount of institutional protection.↩︎

  2. That’s a resource-allocation judgment, not a technical one. And it gets more demanding as the way we work with these tools matures. I think of it as three levels.↩︎

  3. The adage, “[a]nything worth doing is worth doing badly” is much abused. Chesterton was a “distributist” who believed that amateurs, as per the original definition, were the best possible people to undertake an important task.↩︎

  4. A chef-influence I like, “Salt Hank”, started off making content purely about sandwiches. That’s it. Just sandwiches. He opened his own restaurant in 2025.↩︎

  5. The goal isn’t to change the world. The goal is to raise your profile enough that someone eventually says, “I keep seeing this person’s work, and it’s sharp. Let me keep them in mind.” That’s all. Have strong opinions about what’s good enough for you to put your name on. But don’t let the pursuit of perfection stop you from ever starting.↩︎

  6. Experience without Taste produces the veteran manager who ships on time and on budget, and produces nothing anyone wants. Competent execution of the wrong thing, or the mediocre thing, or the thing that technically meets the spec but makes users feel like an afterthought. These people exist in every organization. They’re reliable. They’re also why so many products feel soulless.↩︎

  7. There are entire networks where you can show your side-projects and get help, suggestions, or feedback. YCombinator, the startup incubator, has a forum dedicated to people who want to “Show” what they’ve made.↩︎

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: Who’s Problem Is It Anyway?
item.description:
So My Organization Bought a New Technology…

Every technology is amoral. A spreadsheet does not care whether it tracks charitable donations or wage theft. A database is indifferent to whether it stores medical records responsibly or sells them to advertisers. The software itself has no opinion.

Palantir makes this point at scale. The same platform that helps militaries coordinate in war-zones also enables predictive policing. The underlying technology is, in both cases, a set of data integration and analysis capabilities. Whether those capabilities are pointed at a legitimate military objective or at generating “pre-crime” suspect lists is a question the software cannot ask. People made those decisions. Palantir built the tool and signed the contracts, and the agencies deploying it chose the targets.

The moral weight belongs entirely to the humans on either side of the transaction.

This observation should be obvious, but organizations treat it as if it were controversial, because acknowledging it places the full burden of consequences back on the people making procurement decisions. And those people would rather talk about the technology.

Who Buys vs. Who Operates

The prevailing theory of enterprise technology adoption goes something like this: we have a problem, so we buy a tool to solve it, and then the problem is solved. The theory is clean and satisfying. It also happens to be wrong in a specific and instructive way. What actually happens is that the tool gets purchased to solve a problem as understood by one group of people, then operated by an entirely different group of people, and the distance between those two groups determines most of what follows.

Take Power BI for example. A senior leader decides the organization needs “better visibility into operations.” This is a reasonable desire. It is also, as stated, barely a specification. But a procurement process begins, software is selected, licenses are purchased, and in a few months someone has built a dashboard. The executive now has a screen full of charts that refresh on a schedule. The person who built those charts, and who is now responsible for maintaining the data pipelines behind them, keeping them accurate, responding to requests for new views, and explaining discrepancies when the numbers don’t match someone’s intuition, is typically three or four levels below the person who asked for “visibility” in the first place.

The executive gained a dashboard. The analyst gained a second job.

This pattern recurs with such regularity that it should be treated as a structural feature of how organizations adopt technology, rather than as an unfortunate side effect. CRM systems follow it. Project management platforms follow it. Analytics tools, reporting suites, workflow automation products: in each case, the purchase decision originates with people who want to see, know, or control something, and the operational burden of making the tool function lands on people who had no part in choosing it.

Adoption follows, existing teams are handed new responsibilities, and the search begins for specialists in the new technology.

The Wrong Questions, Answered Precisely

Now, organizations are built on specialization. An executive’s job is to make decisions about where the business is going; an analyst’s job is to prepare the information those decisions require. Asking the VP of Sales to maintain her own data pipelines would be as wasteful as asking the analyst to negotiate contracts. Division of labor exists for a reason, and the reason is that people working within their competence produce better results than people working outside it.

When a manager says “someone has to do the work,” she is stating something true about how complex organizations function. Large institutions cannot operate by committee on every operational question, and the fact that a procurement decision was made three levels above the person who uses the tool does not, by itself, make the decision wrong.

Where this reasoning breaks down is not in its logic but in its completeness. It accounts for the division of labor but fails to account for the division of understanding. Drucker put the matter directly: the most serious mistakes are not made as a result of wrong answers, but as a result of asking the wrong questions.

An executive who asks “How do I get visibility into my operations?” has already made several decisions embedded within the question. He has decided that the problem is informational. He has decided that a visual display will address it. He has decided, implicitly, that the cost of producing and maintaining that display is someone else’s concern. None of these decisions have been examined. They are simply absorbed into the word “visibility” and passed along to procurement. The pragmatist is right that specialization requires delegation. But delegation of execution is one thing; delegation of the problem definition itself is another. The first is sound management. The second is an abdication dressed in the language of efficiency.

The tool, of course, answers whatever question it is configured to answer, and it does so with impressive precision. Whether the question was worth asking is a matter the tool cannot adjudicate. A BI dashboard will show you exactly how many units shipped last quarter, broken out by region, salesperson, and product line. It will not tell you whether shipping volume is the metric that matters, or whether the regional breakdown reflects a meaningful business reality or just the org chart. The dashboard is precise. Precision is not the same as relevance.

The Cost Falls Downward

What makes this a labor problem rather than a technology problem is that the cost of irrelevant precision falls on people who have the least authority to question it. The analyst maintaining the dashboard cannot walk into the VP’s office and suggest that the entire reporting structure is answering questions nobody needs answered. Or rather, she can, but the organizational incentives run against it.

The dashboard exists. It was expensive. People look at it in Monday meetings. Its continued existence is now self-justifying, and the labor required to sustain it is categorized as operational overhead rather than as a decision that could be revisited.

This is where the claim of technology’s amorality becomes important. When an organization says “we implemented Power BI” or “we adopted Salesforce” or “we rolled out Jira,” the passive construction hides the chain of human choices that determined how the tool would be used and who would bear the cost of using it.

The technology did not impose any of this. A BI tool could, in principle, be deployed with the analysts choosing the questions and the executives receiving whatever the analysts determined was relevant. That this arrangement strikes most managers as absurd tells you everything about the actual power structure and nothing about the technology.

Pragmatism as Evasion

There is a version of pragmatism that is genuinely useful. It accepts constraints, works within budgets, ships on deadlines, and does not let the perfect become the enemy of the good. Most functioning organizations run on some measure of it, and a workplace that questioned every assumption before acting would produce nothing at all. The pragmatist who says “we need a reporting tool, let’s pick the best one we can afford and get it running” is doing responsible work.

But there is a second version of pragmatism that merely borrows the first version’s credibility. This version treats the existing distribution of authority as a fixed constraint and optimizes within it, while declining to examine whether that distribution is producing good results. A pragmatic approach to dashboard adoption asks “How do we get this built on time and under budget?” A less common but more useful question would be “What decision will this dashboard change, and is the cost of operating it proportionate to the value of that changed decision?”

The first question sounds responsible. The second question is responsible. They produce very different outcomes. The danger is not pragmatism itself but the ease with which the word can be used to close off inquiry that someone finds inconvenient.

The Tool Without a Domain

Everything described above assumed tools with at least some natural boundary. A BI platform is a reporting tool. A CRM is a customer records tool. These boundaries, however poorly examined, impose a rough constraint on what the tool can be asked to do. The mandate may be vague, but it has a category.

Large language models have no such boundary. An LLM operates on language itself, which means it can be pointed at virtually any task that involves reading, writing, summarizing, analyzing, or generating text. When a manager tells her team to “start using AI to be more productive,” she has issued a mandate with no category at all. She has not identified a problem to be solved or a question to be answered. She has delegated not just the execution, not just the problem definition, but the problem selection. The employee is now responsible for figuring out what the tool should be used for, using it, and producing results that justify the organization’s investment, all while continuing to do the job that existed before the mandate arrived.

The management critique applies here with particular force. A dashboard, at minimum, produces a specific wrong answer that can be examined and argued with. If the BI report says revenue is up twelve percent and a regional manager knows the number is misleading, there is at least a concrete artifact to push back against. An LLM produces fluent prose, and fluent prose is, by its nature, difficult to distinguish from considered thought. When a report reads well, the instinct is to accept it. The question of whether someone thought carefully before producing it, or whether they typed a prompt and cleaned up the output, is not visible in the finished product. The tool’s very competence at generating language becomes an obstacle to evaluating whether the language contains anything worth saying.

This makes the abdication of decision-making to LLMs structurally worse than the BI case.

A productivity mandate issued without specificity was always wasteful; issuing one around a tool that can plausibly fake the appearance of thoughtful work is something different. Organizations that tell their employees to “use AI” without specifying what problem it should address, what quality standard the output must meet, or how the time saved will be reallocated have not made a productivity decision. They have made a discernment decision, and the decision is that discernment is now optional. The tool will produce something. Whether that something required judgment is a question the tool cannot surface, and that the organization, by the structure of its mandate, has chosen not to ask.

The pattern from the BI example holds: the purchase decision flows from the top, the operational burden flows to the bottom, and nobody in between examines whether the right question was asked. But with LLMs, the middle step is harder to see, because the output looks like work regardless of whether work was done.

What the Tool Cannot Answer

The labor crisis in technology adoption is real, but it was not caused by technology. It was caused by people and organizations that use tool procurement as a substitute for thinking clearly about what they need and who should provide it.

Every new platform generates new maintenance work, new reporting obligations, new administrative tasks. This work is real, it takes time, and it is almost always assigned to people who are already busy. That it was not part of the original business case is treated as an oversight rather than as a structural failure of the business case itself. LLMs add a new dimension to this failure: they also generate work that looks, from a distance, like it was already being done, making it easy to assume that the tool is saving time when it may simply be changing what the time is spent on.

A tool, being amoral, will do whatever it is told. The question an organization should ask before purchasing one is not, “What can this tool do?”, but, “What are we going to ask someone to do with it, and is that a good use of their time?”. The first question has a brochure. The second requires judgment.

Questioning usually stops after reading the brochure.

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: The Right to Start a Business
item.description:
The Official Transcript of the Committee on the Socio-Political Fabric

Faithfully Recorded and Annotated for Posterity1

PRELIMINARY NOTES FOR THE RECORD

There is a particular kind of document that humans produce in response to chaos.

Not a solution — solutions require agreement on the nature of the problem, which has been tabled for further review. No: the document produced in response to chaos is a record, a document says: this happened. I was here. I wrote it down.

The document does not say, because it doesn’t need to: *and therefore it is not entirely out of my hands.2

MEMBERS PRESENT

CHAIRMAN NEW WASHING MACHINE (R-IL): A Congressman of the libertarian-adjacent persuasion whose fury at the machinery of the state had, over twelve years in office, become so central to his public identity that it was no longer entirely possible — not for his constituents, not for his staff, and on certain late evenings not entirely for him — to locate where the fury ended and the performance of fury began. He shuffled papers the way other men did push-ups: compulsively, and with the suggestion of unresolved aggression.3

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT (D-DE): A five-term Senator of the variety political scientists describe as a “survivor,” which in this context means something subtly different than it does everywhere else.4 His portfolio was up 22% this quarter, weighted toward domestic surveillance infrastructure and artisanal bunker manufacturers. His tie was the color of bipartisan compromise. He had worn it to four funerals and six fundraising dinners and had somewhere around the third funeral stopped being able to tell the difference.

He had, buried beneath the metrics, a small and underexercised instrument — not quite conscience, not quite regret, something more like a tuning fork struck a long time ago, still faintly vibrating — that occasionally registered something when the metrics weren’t watching. He had learned not to think about it. He was a very accomplished not-thinker-abouter of things. This was, more than any single vote or alliance, the actual engine of his survival.5

WITNESSES

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO, of Brooklyn, New York: Unaffiliated voter. Nonprofit management studies graduate. Son of a venture capitalist, which he had declined to become through an act of will that had felt, at the time, like freedom, and which he was still in the process of determining whether it was. He had a prepared statement, bullet points, and an insight in the second paragraph about closed primary systems that six months of research had produced and that nobody in this room was going to quote.

He also had a faint and stubborn hope. He carried it the way you carry an umbrella in a city where it almost never rains: awkwardly, and unwilling to leave it behind.

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK, of Baton Rouge, Louisiana: Twelve-year hospitality veteran. Driver of a 2009 Chevy Silverado with a cracked windshield that insurance had classified as “cosmetic damage” three times, in language so precisely calibrated to the appearance of reasonableness that she had read each letter twice, certain she’d missed where they were wrong.

She had a manila folder containing three claim denials, two formal appeals, one letter from a state regulator that deployed four paragraphs to say nothing, and a printout of Louisiana House Bill 423 annotated in red pen with words that were, legally speaking, opinions and, morally speaking, verdicts.

She placed the folder on the table and rested both hands flat on top of it.

She did not think of herself as someone who had lost control of her circumstances. She thought of herself as someone who had documented them. This distinction was, for the moment, load-bearing.

THE PROCEEDINGS

CHAIRMAN NEW WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will come to order — using “order” in the loosest possible sense, given that the fundamental ordering principle of this institution appears to be the organized redistribution of resources from people who have less to people who have more, while everyone involved describes it as something else and produces documentation to support the description. I recognize the Senator from Delaware.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Thank you, Mr. Chairman. It is a genuine pleasure to be here.

[He said this the way a man says something he has said so many times the words have worn smooth, like river stones — not false, exactly, just no longer sharp enough to catch on anything.]

The American people—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —are the backbone of this great nation—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: My windshield has a crack in it the size of my forearm and the insurer called it cosmetic.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —and your voices are essential to the health of our democracy—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I have the letters.

[She placed the folder on the table. It landed with the flat authority of something carried a long distance for a specific purpose.]

[The Senator leaned toward his aide.]

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (to aide) Can we get her a sedative? Or a cabinet position in the Department of Agriculture?6

CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Let’s swear in our witnesses and proceed. We have limited time before the next recess, a subsequent recess, and a vote on something that will be described as historic and will not be.

TESTIMONY OF MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: I represent — in the sense that I am one of, not in the sense that anyone appointed me — approximately 1.1 million unaffiliated voters in New York City. The fastest-growing registration category in the state. Also legally barred from participating in the primary elections that decide virtually every meaningful race, because New York operates a closed primary, which means the party you declined to join on principle is the party that decides your representation without you.

I want to be clear about what that does to a person over time. It doesn’t make you angry, exactly. Anger implies a belief that things could be otherwise. What it does is more corrosive. It makes you feel like a category error.

[He paused. This was not in the prepared statement.]

I tried to start a nonprofit. Urban gardening, community food access. The average time to obtain the relevant permits in New York City is fourteen months. The average small business fails within its first year. I am a well-educated person with institutional connections and a father whose network includes three former deputy mayors, and the system still felt like a locked room where all the locks were on the inside.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Wonderful. And you’re from — Queens?

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: Brooklyn.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (nodding in a way that suggested Brooklyn confirmed something he had long suspected about Brooklyn) Mm. I had a very good piece of salmon there in 2019. Great borough.

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: My father’s venture capital firm did an internal analysis last year. The best return on investment in their entire portfolio was regulatory influence. Buying access to permitting agencies. Better than any direct investment in an actual small business. He showed me the numbers. He thought it was funny.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: Well — markets—

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: It was the least funny thing I’ve ever seen presented on a slide.

[A brief silence. The Senator located several things in his notes that required his attention.]

CHAIRMAN NEW WASHING MACHINE: Fourteen months. You know how long it takes to register a business in Estonia?

MR. ZUCCHINI EL GOURDO: I’ve heard—

CHAIRMAN NEW WASHING MACHINE: Eighteen minutes. (produces a printed Wikipedia page) Estonia looked at the administrative state and made a decision. We looked at the administrative state and added a drainage assessment.

TESTIMONY OF PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: Are we done with the polite part?

[She opens the folder.]

I’ve been in hospitality for twelve years. I know food. I know how to hold a line together when it wants to fall apart.

My sister and I have been planning a food truck for three years. Po-boys and smoked boudin. Something ours. So we saved seventeen thousand dollars- we found a truck- we started pulling permits.

[She turned a page. She didn’t need to consult it. The turning was for the record.]

Louisiana requires a state health certificate, a city business license, a fire inspection certification, and proof of a licensed commissary kitchen. You cannot substitute your own kitchen — even commercial-grade — because the certification costs four thousand dollars and takes four to seven months. Then a Department of Revenue seller’s permit, sales tax registration, and in New Orleans a separate French Quarter vendor permit for any street within two blocks that costs twelve hundred dollars and has a waiting list that hasn’t moved since 2022.

Meanwhile: Governor Landry signs “No Pay, No Play.” The $100,000 auto threshold. The Housley Presumption, gone. You tell us it stops frivolous lawsuits. My premiums went up fifteen percent the following quarter. I am currently making decisions about my alternator and my insulin that I should not be making.

Twenty-three percent turnout in the last Louisiana general election. The people who were supposed to be in our corner have spent so long in rooms like this one that they’ve stopped being able to tell the difference between representing us and performing representing us.

[She closed the folder.]

That’s what I wanted to say about the right to start a business.

THE SYNTHESIS7

[A silence formed of the specific kind that accumulates when something true has been said in a space architecturally designed for the management of truth rather than its direct expression. The fluorescent lights hummed. They had hummed through Vietnam and Watergate and the deficit and the collapse and a number of working groups whose white papers were filed in rooms that no longer existed. They had no opinions. This was, in equal measure, a comfort and not a comfort at all.]

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: The commissary kitchen requirement is — yes, that’s something we’ve been actively—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: With what outcome?

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —examining, as part of a broader working group—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: What working group?

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: —that is in the process of—

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I drove seventeen hours.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: (quietly) Yes. You’ve mentioned.

PRINCESS MONSTER TRUCK: I’ll keep mentioning it until something moves.

[The Senator looked at the folder. His second instrument — the tuning fork, the underexercised one — registered something it couldn’t immediately classify. Not guilt. Something prior to guilt. Something that would become guilt if he let it develop.]

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: The working families of this country deserve a regulatory framework that—

[A fraction of a second. The kind of pause that in a different story would be the moment everything changed. He looked, briefly, like a man who had just heard himself — not the words, which were the right words, which were exactly the words the moment required — but the mechanism. The fluency. The phrase arriving fully formed without any intermediate step that could be called deciding.]

[Princess Monster Truck noticed. She didn’t say anything. She just watched.]

[Then the moment passed, back into the general current of things, and he was a five-term Senator again.]

CHAIRMAN NEW WASHING MACHINE: Here is what I keep arriving at.

[He set down his pen.]

You both came here. You know how this system works — or doesn’t — better than most people in this building who are paid to know it. You’re asking the state to move its foot off the hose.8

Instead this issue is going to go to a working group. Then a subcommittee. Then a recess. Then an election cycle. Then a new working group that won’t have read the notes from the old one because those notes are in a format the current document system no longer supports. And somewhere in Louisiana a man who owns a yacht called Tax Shelter will file his quarterly returns in a condition of profound personal comfort, and he will not think about the food truck once.

SENATOR SHMURPLE-BERRY YOGURT: That is an uncharitable characterization of the legislative process.

CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: Is it wrong?

[The lights hummed. Somewhere down the hall, a lobbyist was having an excellent lunch and not thinking about the people who prepared it.]

ADJOURNMENT

CHAIRMAN OLD WASHING MACHINE: The Committee will take these testimonies under advisement. We will produce a white paper. It will be twelve pages. Seven people will read past page four. The rest of the country will continue to stare at the flickering lights of their dwindling bank accounts and draw their own conclusions, several of which will be correct. Meeting adjourned.[^meeting_adjourned]

ADDENDUM: SUBSEQUENT EVENTS

UNDERNEATH MANHATTAN, NY

On the train back to Brooklyn — delayed, indefinitely, for reasons the MTA described as “signal issues” and everyone understood as something much older that nobody had fixed — Zucchini El Gourdo opened his laptop. Not a policy brief. Not the Substack post he’d been unable to finish because he couldn’t find an ending that felt honest rather than performed. A letter. To someone who knew a city councilperson who had been asking, apparently, why the unaffiliated numbers kept climbing and nobody showed up — asking as if she actually wanted to know.

TUSCALOOSA, AL

On the drive back to Louisiana, Princess Monster Truck called her sister.

“We’re still doing it,” she said.

“After all that?”

“Especially after all that. Pull the actual enforcement records on commissary certification — not the statute, the enforcement. I want to know what’s real.”

“You think there’s a gap?”

“There’s always a gap. It’s where we live.”

The crack in the windshield caught the last of the sunset and turned it briefly into something that looked almost like a map.

She drove south, into the dark.

She was, as far as she could tell, the one driving.

The food truck opened in the spring. It was called THE SEVENTEENTH HOUR. The boudin was excellent.

It was not the only one.

Across the country, in the gaps between statutes and enforcement, between the official record and the footnotes, between the theater and the thing the theater was supposed to represent, people were doing the same quiet arithmetic: the cost of asking permission versus the cost of not asking, the distance between the working group and the actual work, the specific weight of a system that processes every genuine thing into the appearance of a genuine thing and files the paperwork correctly and schedules the follow-up meeting and means, on balance, for none of this to change.

And then they were opening food trucks. And mutual aid networks. And neighborhood land trusts. And small schools in living rooms. And local currencies backed by nothing except the agreement of people who had decided to agree. They were building, in the negative space of the official system, the faint outline of a different one — not loudly, not with manifestos, not in ways that would be covered or co-opted or reframed as extremism — just practically, the way people build things when they have stopped waiting for permission and started asking a different question entirely.

The question was not: how do we fix this?

The question was: what do we do instead?

Nobody called it a revolution. Revolutions have press coverage and faces and the kind of drama that gets someone’s name on a stamp. This didn’t have any of that. It had boudin and a waiting list that hadn’t moved since 2022 and eleven thousand dollars saved at eighty dollars a month and a crack in a windshield that caught the light in a way that looked, if you were driving south into the dark and feeling something that wasn’t quite hope but was in the neighborhood, almost like a map.

The working group met for the first time on a Thursday in April. There were eleven members. Three had previously worked for the Louisiana Restaurant Association. One had written the original commissary certification statute in 2004. The agenda was thorough. The minutes would be available upon request.

Nobody requested them.

The lights hummed on.

In the theater, and elsewhere.

Mostly elsewhere.

[END OF TRANSCRIPT]

[Filed with the Office of the Clerk, Committee on the Socio-Political Fabric, 119th Congress]

[White paper status: pending]

[Working group status: convening]

[The boudin: excellent]

[The list the Senator had: somewhere, in a drawer, in a handwriting he no longer entirely recognized as his own]

Footnotes

  1. Posterity has not yet confirmed whether it wanted this.↩︎

  2. Room 2123 smelled of recycled air and the specific ghost of ambition that forms when enough people have sat in the same chairs believing, each in turn, that today was the day something would change. The fluorescent lights hummed at a frequency that could not technically be called despair but whatever feeling it was was renting a studio apartment in the neighborhood and had started leaving its shoes in the hallway.↩︎

  3. The fury was real. It had simply also become, over time, a brand, a platform, a voting bloc, a fundraising apparatus. The fury had acquired stakeholders. It would be unfair to say this had changed nothing. It would be equally unfair to say it hadn’t.↩︎

  4. A “survivor” in the biological sense has endured something that tried to kill them. In the political sense, something that tried to replace them. The skills required are different. The thing you have to be willing to lose, in order to keep going, is different. He had never examined this distinction directly. He had, however, lived it for twenty-three years.↩︎

  5. Whether this constitutes a criticism is left as an exercise for the reader.↩︎

  6. The aide typed something. Nothing happened. Such is the nature of the Department of Agriculture.↩︎

  7. This section is titled “The Synthesis” because the transcript format requires section headers, and “The Part Where Everyone Understands The Problem And Then The Problem Continues” was considered too accurate.↩︎

  8. The speech that followed was one he had given, in various forms, for twelve years. It was true. It had always been true. It arrived, as it always did, exactly as intended — the cadences landing where cadences land when a man has found the version of himself that works and has been working it ever since. He felt, distantly, the faint hum of the machinery. His own machinery. He kept going, because stopping would require explaining why he stopped, and the explanation did not exist in any language the room spoke.↩︎

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

item.title: Event Planning Is Not Innovation
item.description:
One Fine Day,

In The Wedding Planner, Jennifer Lopez plays a woman who has built an entire career orchestrating other people’s weddings. Seating charts are flawless. Timelines are immaculate. The lighting hits just right for those pre-social media photos. She manages emotion, optics, and choreography with military precision.

For those who haven’t seen it: she plans everyone else’s big day because she quietly believes she’s not going to have one of her own, stating, “Those who can’t wed, plan.” So, she perfects the ceremony instead. And then, as was often the case in early 2000’s rom-coms, Matthew McConaughey shows up, complications ensue, and — all the while — the movie reminds us of something simple: planning a wedding is not the same thing as building a marriage.

If you squint, a lot of university “innovation ecosystems” look eerily similar.

You have mastered the ceremony. Pitch nights sell out. Demo days are standing room only. Calendars are packed. LinkedIn is thriving. The choreography is precise, the optics are polished, the programming tight. But months later, you look up and realize nothing meaningful has been committed — no capital, no customers, no real company.

Beautiful ceremony. No marriage.

“Those who can’t wed, plan.” In some corners of university commercialization, it feels more like those who can’t commercialize, program.

Busy Is Not Productive

This failure has almost nothing to do with talent or idea quality, and nearly everything to do with a system designed to generate motion without a reliable mechanism for carrying work from one decision point to another.

Universities are extremely good at producing activity. Applications are counted. Mentors are logged. Cohorts are launched. Attendance stays up. Engagement stays high, and the photos evince the attendee’s status as LinkedIn royalty!

If the answer is nothing changed except the calendar, then you’ve planned a wedding without vows.

But underneath that flurry of activity, the technologies sit in roughly the same place they were before — neither meaningfully advanced nor decisively stopped. They just are. They’re present. On the slide. In the portfolio. Stuck between gates.

This is like getting a flat tire and convening a panel of experts in rubber compounds, axle alignment, optimal PSI, and lug-nut metallurgy — listening to their insights on best practices for tire performance within the broader automotive ecosystem — instead of just changing the tire.

From Activity to Judgment

Universities love pipelines because pipelines feel fair — and because they come with a comforting logic: put enough things in, keep them moving, and something good is bound to come out the other end. It sounds rational. It sounds efficient.

It sounds like progress. It isn’t.

Commercialization is not a conveyor belt. It’s not forward motion by default. It’s a series of uncomfortable, explicit decisions made under uncertainty. It requires someone — or some system — to look at the evidence and say: this moves forward. This doesn’t. This goes here. This stops.

Commercialization is a set of gated decisions under uncertainty:

  • Is this worth advancing?
  • Which pathway makes sense right now — license, startup, strategic partnership, or stop?
  • What is the next value inflection point?
  • Who has to believe what, by when, for this to work?
  • And who, specifically, is going to pay for it?

A serious commercialization system is designed to answer those questions repeatedly, quickly, and with evidence.

And this is where some university innovation activity tends to miss the mark.

It’s easier to schedule the next workshop than it is to force a meaningful decision. It’s safer to move a project into the next cohort than to decide whether it should exist at all. Activity feels productive, it generates updates, it fills dashboards, and it fools everyone into feeling engaged.

When a university treats programming and activity as an outcomes, everything becomes a KPI. Everything counts as progress. The pitch night counts. The fireside chat counts. The networking cocktail hour counts.

And because all of these count, no one bothers to ask the harder questions:

  • What risk was reduced?
  • What belief was validated?
  • What capital was committed?
  • What changed?

If the answer is nothing changed except the calendar, then you’ve planned a wedding without vows — beautifully staged, masterfully orchestrated, and fundamentally empty.

Progress Over Production Value

There are commercialization/innovation systems that tilt toward decisions and outcomes — rather than optics and attendance.

For example, there are dedicated proof-of-concept funding models that lean on staged, evidence-based gates. Programs, such as the NIH Proof-of-Concept Centers1, require projects to tie funding to defined technical and commercial milestones — actual prototypes, feasibility data, and market engagement — before advancement is allowed.

Support isn’t awarded because a workshop was full; it’s awarded because real evidence of commercial potential exists AND risk has been meaningfully reduced. This kind of gate-driven progression tends to produce more actual licensing outcomes, not just piles of post-event clickbait.

That’s what gating looks like. That’s decision infrastructure.

Decisions, Decisions, Decisions,

Technology Transfer, Technology Development, and Technology Commercialization Offices (TTOs/OTD/OTCs) inside universities are supposed to serve the same role — not as programming machines but as decision makers.

A 2024 study exploring the current state of technology transfer in the U.S.2 finds that offices that emphasize structured engagement and market connection strategies tend to be more effective in bridging research and markets than those that don’t. For example, of those high preforming offices:

  • ~35% use customized licensing agreements tailored to specific industry needs,
  • ~30% have collaborative research ventures to align academic output with commercial partners’ most pressing issue,
  • and ~20% have industry-sponsored research programs that embed external incentives early,

Some offices have even redesigned the licensing gate itself1. A handful of TTOs, like the University of Cincinnati3 and the University of Missouri System4, have developed express licensing mechanisms — standardized, take-it-or-leave-it agreements with predefined terms — to cut out negotiation drag and get technologies into industry hands faster. These aren’t fancy events with cranberry baked brie bites — they’re structural interventions designed to force a decision about whether a technology will move forward or not, without weeks of back-and-forth over terms.

The Association of University Technology Managers (AUTM) 2024 Licensing Activity Survey5 demonstrates real commercialization outcomes tell the most meaningful story. Research institutions continued to navigate funding shifts and strategic uncertainty while producing measurable market outcomes.

U.S. research expenditures reached roughly $109 billion, underlining the scale of raw innovation output. Invention disclosures continued to climb; and institutions reported steady startup formation and new products entering the market during the reporting period. Universities reported:

  • 26,196 invention disclosures
  • 14,432 new U.S. patent applications filed

— the idea machine is humming. But that’s only half the story. The real signal shows up here:

  • 9,507 licenses and options executed,
  • 775 new products created, and
  • 951 startups formed.

That’s the difference between programming and productivity. One fills seats. The other produces outcomes.

When a TTO builds structured decision points — milestones that reduce technical risk, demonstrate market interest, and negotiate commitment — it makes progress. The AUTM survey5 shows that offices leaning into those practices continue generating startups, licenses, and other real, quantifiable outcomes.

The Pitch Day Trap

If your commercialization system treats packed pitch rooms and demo nights as the success metric, you’ve lost the plot.

In many TTO/OTC-linked commercialization ecosystems, pitch competitions and showcase events become the de-facto milestones — the things you build calendars around, measure “engagement” on, and post about afterward. But if you treat that as progress, if you treat stage presence and applause as alignment, you’re optimizing for performance, not outcomes.

When a university treats programming and activity as an outcomes, everything becomes a KPI.

An effective pitch shouldn’t be just a god performance. Pitching is supposed to come after the hard questions about what comes next and why have been answered:

  • What is the next commercialization decision, and what evidence clears it?
  • What proof of market interest have you gathered?
  • What experiments remain, and how quickly can you run them?
  • What is your stopping rule — the point at which you decide to pivot or halt?
  • What commitment are you asking for, specifically, and why now?

A field study of pitch training — where would-be founders were randomly assigned to receive training or not and then tracked over time — found that training does indeed improve pitch delivery and increases participation in future competitions and related programming6.

Participants worked on their pitches longer and appeared in more events and accelerators afterward. But those gains in presentation quality and participation did not reliably translate into stronger, long-term outcomes like hiring employees or sustaining ventures; in fact, those who received pitch training were sometimes more likely to abandon their initial ventures.

In plain English: better slides and a confident stage presence will get a presenter into more demo nights and more pitch rooms. They do not automatically make your venture more investable, more viable, or more likely to survive in the marketplace.

When commercialization systems reward strong stage presence over timely decision-making, they end up optimizing for packed pitch rooms, high attendance counts, and social media engagement instead of optimizing for what really matters: licenses executed, capital committed, partnerships formed, market captured, and ventures launched.

If your KPI is how many teams stood on stage and burped their way through a slide deck at whatever Showcase last fall, you’ll get pitches. You’ll get a lot of them.
